name: Stanford Prison Full
description: The Stanford Prison Experiment (SPE) was a 1971 social psychology study conducted at Stanford University. It aimed to investigate the psychological effects of perceived power, focusing on the struggle between prisoners and prison officers. Participants, selected for their psychological stability, were randomly assigned to roles as either prisoners or guards in a mock prison environment. The experiment, initially planned for two weeks, was terminated after only six days due to the rapidly escalating psychological distress and unexpectedly intense behavior exhibited by the participants. Proponents of the SPE often cite it as powerful evidence of the situational power of social roles and the inherent dangers of unchecked authority. They argue it demonstrates how easily ordinary people can be induced to behave in cruel and dehumanizing ways when placed in positions of power. The study has been used to explain instances of abuse in real-world prison settings and other hierarchical institutions. However, the SPE has faced significant criticism and re-evaluation in recent years. Critics argue that the experiment lacked scientific rigor, citing issues such as demand characteristics (participants behaving as they believed the experimenters wanted them to), selection bias (the initial screening process may have inadvertently selected individuals predisposed to certain behaviors), and the active role of the lead researcher, Philip Zimbardo, in influencing the guards' behavior. Some researchers argue that the SPE was more of a theatrical performance than a scientifically valid study, questioning the generalizability of its findings. Recent analyses of archival materials suggest Zimbardo actively encouraged harsh treatment by the guards, further undermining the claims of spontaneous emergent behavior. The 'Lucifer Effect,' popularized by Zimbardo, stems from this experiment.
subtitle: The Power of Social Roles

claims.claim: Situational factors can override individual disposition in determining behavior.
claims.evidence: The rapid shift in behavior of participants assigned to guard and prisoner roles.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that pre-existing personality traits and demand characteristics played a larger role than situational factors alone.

claims.claim: Power corrupts, and absolute power corrupts absolutely.
claims.evidence: The guards' increasingly authoritarian and abusive behavior towards the prisoners.
claims.criticism: Not all guards exhibited abusive behavior, and the experiment's design may have encouraged such behavior.

claims.claim: The SPE demonstrates the potential for abuse inherent in hierarchical systems.
claims.evidence: The ease with which participants adopted their assigned roles and engaged in dehumanizing behavior.
claims.criticism: The artificiality of the experimental setting limits the generalizability of these findings to real-world situations.

keyFigures.name: Philip Zimbardo
keyFigures.role: Lead researcher of the Stanford Prison Experiment

keyFigures.name: Carlo Prescott
keyFigures.role: Ex-convict who assisted with the experiment's design, later claimed manipulation

keyFigures.name: Ben Blum
keyFigures.role: Journalist who investigated and critiqued the SPE

keyFigures.name: Thibault Le Texier
keyFigures.role: Historian who deconstructed the SPE's methodology

mythologyConnections: The Ring of Gyges (Plato's Republic): Invisibility grants unchecked power
Concepts of justice and morality tested under duress

timeline.event: SPE used as a case study in psychology and criminology
Ben Blum publishes critique of SPE in 'Medium'
Thibault Le Texier publishes 'Histoire d'un mensonge' (History of a Lie), further critiquing the SPE
